revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:羽黒権現]] {{Infobox deity|venerated_in={{ill|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|1=Shugendō}}}}
'''Haguro Gongen''' (羽黒権現) is a syncretic deity in Japan associated with the mountain worship and Shugendo traditions of {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|qid=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province|1=Dewa Province}} and {{ill|Mount Haguro|en|Mount Haguro|qid=Q3090765|lt=Mount Haguro|1=Mount Haguro}}. Based on the [[honji suijaku]] doctrine, Haguro Gongen is considered a manifestation of the bodhisattva [[:en:Shō Kannon|Shō Kannon Bosatsu]] (Sacred Avalokitesvara Bodhisattva) appearing in a temporary, earthly form ("gongen"). Also known as Haguro Daigongen or Mount Haguro Daigongen, it is one of the deities collectively referred to as the '''Three Gongen of Dewa''' (出羽三所大権現). Together with [[Gassan Daigongen]] and [[Yudonosan Daigongen]], the three were historically referred to as the Three Mountains of Haguro or Ushū Three Mountains, which became prominent training grounds for Shugendo alongside {{ill|Mount Ōmine|en|Mount Ōmine|qid=Q3695989|lt=Mount Ōmine|1=Mount Ōmine}} and {{ill|Mount Hiko|en|Mount Hiko|qid=Q3077886|lt=Mount Hiko|1=Mount Hiko}}. The main hall (Daido) of Jakkoji Temple on the summit of Mount Haguro, now known as the Sankan Gosaiden, enshrined the honjibutsu of the three mountains (Shō Kannon Bosatsu, [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], and [[:en:Dainichi Nyorai|Dainichi Nyorai]]) until the "Shinbutsu Bunri" (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) during the Meiji Restoration in 1868. This separation resulted in the abolition of the gongen title, the destruction or relocation of Buddhist images, and the reorganization of Jakkoji Temple into the present-day {{ill|Dewa Shrine|en|Dewa Shrine|qid=Q11395802|lt=Dewa Shrine|1=Dewa Shrine}}. Today, the collective name {{ill|Three Mountains of Dewa|en|Three Mountains of Dewa|qid=Q1155726|lt=Dewa Sanzan Shrine|1=Three Mountains of Dewa}} refers to the {{ill|Dewa Shrine|en|Dewa Shrine|qid=Q11395802|lt=Dewa Shrine|1=Dewa Shrine}}, [[Gassan Shrine]], and {{ill|Yudonosan Shrine|en|Yudonosan Shrine|qid=Q11563607|lt=Yudonosan Shrine|1=Yudonosan Shrine}}. However, the term "Dewa Sanzan" only gained widespread use in the early Shōwa period. The [[:en:Shinbutsu Bunri|Shinbutsu Bunri]] policies and subsequent persecution of Buddhism ([[Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu Kishaku]]) led to significant changes in the syncretic faith. Many existing Haguro Shrines across Japan were originally dedicated to Haguro Gongen.
== Origin == According to tradition, the third prince of {{ill|Emperor Sushun|en|Emperor Sushun|qid=Q349238|lt=Emperor Sushun|1=Emperor Sushun}}, {{ill|Prince Hachiko|en|Prince Hachiko|qid=Q7244045|lt=Prince Hachiko|1=Prince Hachiko}}, was guided by a three-legged spirit crow to the Ako Valley on Mount Haguro, where he experienced the divine power of Shō Kannon Bosatsu and established the mountain as a sacred site. During the medieval period, the deity manifested as Haguro Daigongen. However, following the Shinbutsu Bunri reforms, Buddhist elements were removed, and Prince Hachiko's establishment of the shrine was emphasized, as recorded in the Edo-period "Haguro San Engi" (Chronicles of Mount Haguro).
== Shinbutsu Bunri and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]]'s Shinbutsu Bunri and Haibutsu Kishaku policies led to the suppression of Shugendo and the abolition of Haguro Gongen as a deity.<ref name="douran">Shirō Usui, "The Turmoil of the Separation of Shinto and Buddhism" (神仏分離の動乱), Shibunkaku Publishing, December 2004, ISBN 978-4784212187</ref> Jakkoji Temple on Mount Haguro was abolished and forcibly reorganized into {{ill|Dewa Shrine|en|Dewa Shrine|qid=Q11395802|lt=Dewa Shrine|1=Dewa Shrine}} (Ideha Jinja).<ref name='douran'/> Most Haguro Gongen shrines nationwide were converted into [[Shinto]] Haguro Shrines or Dewa Shrines.
== Haguro Shugendo == Few historical records survive regarding '''Haguro Shugendo''' during the medieval period. Under the leadership of Betsu Ten’yū, Haguro Shugendo became a direct affiliate of [[:en:Kanei-ji|Kanei-ji]] in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, enjoying the protection of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. Haguro Daigongen, Gassan Daigongen, and Yudonosan Daigongen were recognized as the '''Three Gongen of Dewa''', serving as the religious and spiritual guardians of 33 eastern provinces, rivaling the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] in the west.
While the Shinbutsu Bunri policies heavily impacted Haguro Shugendo, it survived as a distinct practice. Today, the [[Haguro Shugendo Honshu]] organization, based at Shōzen-in Temple in Tōge, continues the tradition independently of Shinto authorities.<ref>Joseph Cali and John Dougill, "Shugendo Practices of Mount Haguro," translated by Masataka Suzuki, Kobundo, May 1985, ISBN 978-4335160097</ref><ref>Jun Miyae, "Haguro Shugendo – Its History and Pilgrimage," Iwata Shoin, October 2000, ISBN 978-4872941807</ref>. Each August, the autumn peak pilgrimage (Mineiri) at {{ill|Kōtaku-ji|ja|荒沢寺|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtaku-ji|1=Kōtaku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11618770}} includes practices such as the Ten Realms Training (Jikkai Shugyō), the burning self-purification rite (Shintō Goma), and the South Sea Incense Rite.<ref>Documentary film "Shugen: The Autumn Peak of Mount Haguro" directed by Minoru Kitamura.</ref>
== Temples Enshrining Haguro Gongen == A few temples survived the Haibutsu Kishaku and continue to enshrine Haguro Gongen: * Chōmeizan Sasanodera (米沢市, Yamagata Prefecture)
== References == {{reflist|2}}
